1_dma_impact_on_consent.overview: The EU Digital Markets Act (Regulation 2022/1925) became fully enforceable March 6, 2024. It designates 'gatekeepers' and imposes strict consent and data-combining rules that directly affect how advertisers operate.

key_requirements_for_gatekeepers.article_5_2: Gatekeepers must NOT combine personal data from their core platform service with data from other services or third-party sources WITHOUT explicit user consent under GDPR.
key_requirements_for_gatekeepers.article_5_2_consent_withdrawal: Consent withdrawal must be as easy as granting consent, with immediate effect across all gatekeeper services.
key_requirements_for_gatekeepers.article_6_10: Gatekeepers must not use personal data of business users' end users for their own advertising unless consented.

google.consent_signals_required: Since March 2024, Google requires advertisers to pass consent signals for EEA/UK/Switzerland users via Consent Mode v2. Users who opt out are non-attributable.
google.google_ads_impact: Without valid consent signals, conversions from EEA users are not attributed, remarketing audiences are not built, and Enhanced Conversions cannot process user data.
google.consent_mode_v2_mandatory: Google Consent Mode v2 with ad_user_data and ad_personalization parameters is mandatory for EEA traffic.

meta.pay_or_consent_model: Meta's 'pay or consent' model was found in breach of DMA by EC preliminary findings (July 2024). Meta fined €200M (April 2025) for not giving users a less-personalized-data option.
meta.consent_requirement: Advertisers must pass explicit consent signals through Meta Pixel and Conversions API for EU users.

apple.att_framework: App Tracking Transparency (ATT) requires apps to request permission before tracking across apps/websites owned by other companies.
apple.fine: Apple fined €500M (April 2025) for DMA non-compliance regarding App Store steering rules.

advertiser_obligations: Implement a compliant CMP that captures granular consent
Pass consent signals to all gatekeeper platforms (Google Consent Mode, Meta consent parameters)
Maintain audit trail/proof of consent collection
Ensure consent withdrawal is operationally as easy as granting
Do not combine cross-platform user data without explicit consent

2_google_consent_mode_v2.overview: Google Consent Mode v2 is a framework that adjusts Google tag behavior based on user consent status. It communicates consent state to Google services via URL parameters.

ad_storage.description: Controls storage (cookies) related to advertising (e.g., Google Ads _gcl_* cookies)
ad_storage.when_denied: No advertising cookies are set. Cookieless pings still sent (Advanced mode). Ads conversion linker cookie (_gcl_aw) not written.

analytics_storage.description: Controls storage (cookies) related to analytics (e.g., GA4 _ga, _ga_* cookies)
analytics_storage.when_denied: No analytics cookies set. Cookieless pings sent with consent state info. No client_id or session_id persisted.

ad_user_data.description: Controls whether user data can be SENT to Google for advertising measurement (conversion tracking, Enhanced Conversions)
ad_user_data.when_denied: User data (email, phone, address) not sent to Google for ads measurement. Conversion data sent but without user identifiers.

ad_personalization.description: Controls whether user data can be used for personalized advertising (remarketing, similar audiences)
ad_personalization.when_denied: No remarketing audiences built, no personalized ads served to this user. Sets NPA (Non-Personalized Ads) flag.

functionality_storage.description: Controls storage for website functionality (e.g., language preferences, UI settings)
functionality_storage.note: Google tags do not use this; it is for CMP/custom tag integration.

personalization_storage.description: Controls storage related to personalization (e.g., content recommendations)
personalization_storage.note: Google tags do not use this; it is for CMP/custom tag integration.

security_storage.description: Controls storage related to security (e.g., authentication, fraud prevention)
security_storage.note: Always granted as it is essential for security functions.

basic.description: Tags are completely blocked until consent is granted. No data sent to Google before consent.
basic.modeling: Conversion modeling available in Google Ads. NO behavioral modeling in GA4.
basic.data_loss: Higher data loss since no cookieless pings are sent.

advanced.description: Tags load with default 'denied' state. Cookieless pings are sent. Tags adjust behavior when consent is granted/denied.
advanced.modeling: Conversion modeling in Google Ads AND behavioral modeling in GA4.
advanced.data_loss: Lower data loss due to cookieless pings feeding machine learning models.

cookieless_pings.description: In Advanced mode, when consent is denied, Google tags send minimal, cookieless pings to Google servers.

types.consent_state_ping: Sent on every page where Consent Mode is active. Communicates granted/denied status for each consent type.
types.conversion_ping: Sent when a conversion event occurs but ad_storage is denied. Contains no user identifiers, no cookies. Used for aggregate conversion modeling.
types.analytics_ping: Sent for GA4 events when analytics_storage is denied. Contains page URL, timestamp, user agent, but no _ga cookie. No client_id persisted.

conversion_modeling.how_it_works: Google AI analyzes observable data from consented users and uses historical trends to model conversions for unconsented users.
requirements: Consent Mode must be active for at least 7 days
Minimum daily traffic thresholds (varies by platform)
Google Ads: at least 1,000 ad-click events per day with consent denied for 7+ days
GA4: behavioral modeling requires Advanced mode and sufficient daily events
privacy_safeguards: Never used to track individual users across sites
Never used to build remarketing lists
Never used to generate individual user profiles
Based on aggregate statistical models only

gcs.description: Google Consent State. Encodes ad_storage and analytics_storage.

values.G100: Both ad_storage and analytics_storage denied (Advanced mode only)

gcd.description: Google Consent Default. Encodes all four consent signals (ad_storage, analytics_storage, ad_user_data, ad_personalization) with information about default state and updates.
gcd.note: Present in every Google tag request regardless of Consent Mode enablement. More complex encoding than gcs.

gtag_implementation_code.default_denied: gtag('consent', 'default', { ad_storage: 'denied', analytics_storage: 'denied', ad_user_data: 'denied', ad_personalization: 'denied', wait_for_update: 500 });
gtag_implementation_code.region_specific_defaults: gtag('consent', 'default', { ad_storage: 'denied', analytics_storage: 'denied', ad_user_data: 'denied', ad_personalization: 'denied', region: ['IT', 'FR', 'DE', 'ES'] });
gtag_implementation_code.wait_for_update_note: wait_for_update (in ms) tells Google tags to wait for a consent update before firing. Recommended 500ms to allow CMP to initialize.

3_gdpr_enforcement_trends_2025.overview: 2025 saw record enforcement activity, particularly around consent banners, dark patterns, and cookie compliance.

major_fines.reason: Promotional ads in Gmail without prior consent. Consent UI designs that steered users toward personalized ads (accept = 1 click, reject = navigate to settings).
Cookies installed before user consent. Banners lacked adequate transparency. 'Reject all' did not function correctly. Dark patterns: reject buried behind multiple clicks.
Pay-or-consent model not giving users a less-data-intensive option.
Illegal collection of biometric data without consent.

cumulative_stats.total_fines_to_date: €5.88 billion across 2,245+ recorded penalties by end of 2025
cumulative_stats.consent_related_share: 3 of 5 largest 2025 fines (totaling €475M) involved cookie consent violations

dark_patterns.description: Design tricks that nudge users toward consenting
examples: Accept all = prominent single button; Reject = hidden behind settings
Asymmetric click paths (1 click to accept, 3+ to reject)
Confusing language that obscures the reject option
dark_patterns.regulatory_position: CNIL, EDPB, and national DPAs unanimously consider these non-compliant. Reject must be as easy as Accept.

cookie_walls.description: Blocking content access unless cookies are accepted
cookie_walls.regulatory_position: Generally prohibited unless a genuine equivalent alternative (without tracking) is offered. EDPB Opinion 5/2020.

legitimate_interest_abuse.description: Claiming legitimate interest for advertising/tracking instead of seeking consent
legitimate_interest_abuse.regulatory_position: Advertising and profiling almost never qualify for legitimate interest. EDPB guidelines emphasize consent as the only valid legal basis for non-essential cookies/tracking under ePrivacy Directive.

prior_consent_enforcement.description: Cookies/trackers firing before user gives consent
prior_consent_enforcement.regulatory_position: Strictly prohibited. Any non-essential cookie or tracker must not be placed before valid consent is obtained.

4_eprivacy_by_country.overview: The ePrivacy Directive (2002/58/EC) is implemented differently by each EU member state. Key differences affect cookie consent, analytics exemptions, and consent duration.

italy_garante.authority: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali
italy_garante.key_guidelines: Linee guida cookie e altri strumenti di tracciamento (June 10, 2021, effective January 9, 2022)
italy_garante.scroll_as_consent: EXPLICITLY PROHIBITED. Scrolling alone cannot constitute valid consent. Only exception: scrolling as part of a broader series of unambiguous actions.

analytics_exemption.first_party_analytics: May be placed WITHOUT consent if used for aggregate statistics on a single site/app.
third_party_analytics_conditions: IP address masked (at least 1/4 of IPv4, equivalent for IPv6)
Data not combined with other user information
Data not shared with third parties
Used only for aggregate statistics for a single site/app
analytics_exemption.google_analytics_note: GA4 with IP anonymization and restricted data sharing CAN qualify as exempt if all conditions met. However, cross-site measurement disqualifies it.

cookie_banner_requirements: First layer: must contain brief description of purposes + Accept/Reject buttons of equal prominence
Second layer: detailed information about each cookie category with granular choices
Technical cookies need not require consent but must be described
'X' close button on banner = deny consent (does NOT equal acceptance)
Re-presentation of banner: no sooner than 6 months after user makes a choice

france_cnil.authority: Commission nationale de l'informatique et des libertés (CNIL)

conditions: Limited to measuring audience of the site only (not cross-site)
Does not allow tracking persons across different sites
Not combined with other processing or data
Cookie lifetime limited to 13 months, data retention limited to 25 months
analytics_exemption.exempt_tools: Matomo (with specific configuration), AT Internet, and similar first-party tools. Google Analytics does NOT qualify for exemption per CNIL.

specific_rules: Reject must be available at the same level/click depth as Accept
Continue browsing does NOT constitute consent
Cookie walls generally prohibited unless genuine free alternative offered
Consent proof must be maintained (audit trail)

germany_dsk.authority: Datenschutzkonferenz (DSK, Conference of German Data Protection Authorities)
germany_dsk.legal_framework: TTDSG §25 implements ePrivacy cookie rules into German law. Consent required for any storage/access to terminal equipment unless strictly necessary.

analytics_exemption.conditions: Analytics cookies exempt only if NO personal data is processed at all. Stricter than France/Italy.
analytics_exemption.note: In practice, most analytics tools require consent under German law since IP addresses are personal data per German interpretation.

specific_rules: PIMS (Personal Information Management Services) recognized as valid consent intermediaries under TTDSG §26
Consent banners must be accessible and non-manipulative
State-level DPAs can and do conduct systematic website audits
Several Länder DPAs have sent mass non-compliance letters to websites

spain_aepd.authority: Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD)
spain_aepd.key_guidelines: Guía sobre el uso de las cookies (November 2019, updated 2023)

analytics_exemption.conditions: First-party, aggregated, internal-use-only measurement can operate without consent under similar conditions to France.

specific_rules: Cookie policy must be accessible from every page of the website
Second-layer information must include identity of data controller, purposes, retention periods, and recipients
Implied consent from continued browsing is NOT valid
AEPD has been less aggressive in enforcement compared to CNIL/Garante but increasing scrutiny

5_italian_garante_cookie_guidelines_deta.official_reference: Provvedimento del 10 giugno 2021 [9677876], published in Gazzetta Ufficiale July 9, 2021
5_italian_garante_cookie_guidelines_deta.scope: All websites and apps targeting Italian users or operated from Italy

must_contain: Brief description that site uses technical cookies and (if applicable) profiling cookies
Link to full cookie policy (second layer)
Reject button (equal visual prominence to Accept)
Link to granular cookie preference settings
must_not: Make Accept visually dominant over Reject
first_layer.x_close_behavior: Closing the banner with X = deny non-essential cookies. Technical cookies only remain active.

must_contain: Full list of cookies by category (technical, analytics, profiling)
For each cookie: name, purpose, duration, first/third party

scroll_prohibition.rule: Scrolling the page does NOT constitute valid consent.
scroll_prohibition.rationale: Scroll generates too many false positives. Random user actions must not be misinterpreted as affirmative consent.
scroll_prohibition.exception: Only if scrolling is embedded in a broader sequence of clearly affirmative actions (extremely narrow exception, practically never applicable).

first_party_technical_analytics.conditions: Must be used only for aggregate statistics of a single site/app. No user identification possible.

third_party_analytics_as_technical.consent_required: Not required IF all conditions met
conditions: IP address truncation (mask at least 1/4 of IPv4)
No sharing with third parties for their own purposes

profiling_analytics.note: Any analytics that enables user-level tracking, cross-site measurement, or feeds advertising systems requires full consent.

consent_storage_and_renewal.consent_proof: Controller must be able to demonstrate that valid consent was obtained (audit trail requirement under GDPR Art. 7.1)
consent_storage_and_renewal.renewal_period: 6 months. Do not re-present banner before 6 months unless cookie policy changes.
consent_storage_and_renewal.consent_record: Must store: timestamp, version of cookie policy consented to, granular choices made, method of consent.

penalties_for_non_compliance.administrative_fines: Up to €20 million or 4% of annual worldwide turnover (GDPR Art. 83)
penalties_for_non_compliance.garante_enforcement: Garante conducts both complaint-driven and proactive investigations. Has issued corrective orders and fines to major Italian websites.

6_meta_conversions_api_consent.overview: Meta Conversions API (CAPI) is a server-to-server integration. It does NOT bypass consent requirements—consent must be checked before sending events.

browser_side.meta_pixel: fbq('consent', 'revoke') to deny, fbq('consent', 'grant') to allow. Must be called BEFORE fbq('init').

server_side_capi.approach: Check consent status in your server/data layer BEFORE making CAPI calls. Do not send events for users who have not consented.

values.empty_array: [] = no restrictions (user consented or not subject to restrictions)
values.ldu: ['LDU'] = Limited Data Use mode for US state privacy laws (CCPA/CPRA)
values.ldu_with_geo: ['LDU'], country=1, state=1000 = LDU with automatic geolocation

data_processing_options.note: LDU is for US state laws only. For GDPR, the solution is to NOT send the event at all if consent is not given.

required_parameters_per_event: Hashed user data (email, phone, etc.) - only if consented

limited_data_use_ldu.purpose: Compliance with US state privacy laws (California CCPA/CPRA, Virginia VCDPA, Colorado CPA, etc.)
limited_data_use_ldu.not_for_gdpr: LDU does NOT satisfy GDPR. For EU users without consent, do not send data at all.
what_ldu_restricts: Data not used for personalized advertising
Data not used for optimization beyond the specific advertiser

meta_consent_mode_2025.description: Meta now supports a consent mode similar to Google's, integrating with CMPs.
meta_consent_mode_2025.record_keeping: 2025 policy update: advertisers must maintain detailed records of user consent including when, where, and how consent was obtained. Must provide evidence to Meta upon request.

integration_with_gtm_server_side.approach: CMP sets consent in dataLayer → web GTM passes consent state to server GTM → server GTM checks consent before firing Meta CAPI tag
integration_with_gtm_server_side.meta_tag_in_sgtm: The Meta Conversions API tag in server-side GTM should be configured to respect consent settings. Use consent-based trigger conditions.

gdpr_compliance_checklist: Do NOT fire Meta Pixel before consent is granted
Do NOT send CAPI events for EU users who have not consented
Hash all user data (SHA-256) before sending
Implement fbq('consent', 'revoke') as default for EU users
Pass data_processing_options for US users subject to state laws
Support consent withdrawal (stop sending data when consent is revoked)

7_server_side_tagging_consent.overview: Server-side GTM does not automatically enforce consent. Consent state must be forwarded from the web container and explicitly checked in the server container.

web_to_server_flow: 1. User interacts with CMP on website
2. CMP pushes consent state to dataLayer
3. Web GTM gtag/GA4 tag reads consent state
4. Google tags append consent parameters (gcs, gcd) to HTTP request sent to server container
5. Server container's GA4 client extracts gcs/gcd from request
6. Consent state is available in server container event data

x_ga_gcs_parameter.description: In server-side GTM event data, the consent state is available as x-ga-gcs
x_ga_gcs_parameter.format: Same as gcs: G1xy where x=ad_storage, y=analytics_storage

x_ga_gcd_parameter.description: Extended consent data for all four consent signals

server_side_consent_enforcement.built_in_behavior: Native Google tags (GA4, Google Ads, Floodlight) in server GTM automatically read GCS and respect consent without extra config.

third_party_tags.problem: Non-Google tags in server GTM (Meta CAPI, TikTok, custom HTTP requests) do NOT automatically respect consent. You must build consent checks.

solution.step_1: Create an Event Data variable for x-ga-gcs (Key Path: x-ga-gcs)
solution.step_2: Create a trigger condition: x-ga-gcs matches RegEx G1[1][1] (both granted) or appropriate pattern
solution.step_3: Attach this trigger to third-party tags so they only fire when consent is granted

common_pitfalls: Assuming server-side GTM inherently blocks unconsented data (it does NOT)
Not forwarding consent state from web container to server container
Firing Meta CAPI or other third-party tags without consent checks in server GTM
Using server-side tagging as a way to 'bypass' consent (this is illegal and auditable)
Not checking x-ga-gcs before custom HTTP request tags
audit_checks: Verify x-ga-gcs is present in server-side event data
Verify third-party server tags have consent-based triggers
Test: reject all cookies in CMP → check if server-side tags still fire to third parties
Check server-side GTM tag configuration for consent settings

8_common_compliance_failures.overview: Technical patterns frequently found in audits that indicate non-compliance. Statistics: 60% of websites with 'Reject all' do not honor the choice; 72% of financial services sites set non-essential cookies before consent; 74% of ecommerce sites collect behavioral data on page load.

tags_firing_before_consent.description: Marketing/analytics tags execute before the CMP initializes or before the user makes a choice
technical_indicators: Network requests to google-analytics.com, facebook.com/tr, doubleclick.net visible in DevTools before CMP banner appears
Hardcoded scripts in <head> that load before the CMP script
gtag('config', ...) called without prior gtag('consent', 'default', ...) command
fbq('track', 'PageView') firing without prior fbq('consent', 'revoke')
tags_firing_before_consent.root_cause: CMP loads at ~2 seconds, but tracking pixels (Facebook, LinkedIn, etc.) are optimized and fire at ~0.5 seconds.

hardcoded_tracking_scripts.description: Marketing scripts embedded directly in HTML rather than loaded through GTM with consent triggers
technical_indicators: <script> tags in page source loading tracking domains directly
Third-party scripts not managed by tag manager
Inline JavaScript that sets tracking cookies

consent_not_honored_on_reject.description: User clicks 'Reject All' but cookies are still set or network requests still fire
technical_indicators: After rejecting: _ga, _gid, _fbp, _gcl_au cookies still present in Application > Cookies
Network requests to ad/analytics domains still visible after rejection
dataLayer still contains marketing events after rejection

missing_consent_default_command.description: Google Consent Mode default command not present or fires after tags
technical_indicators: In Console: dataLayer has no 'consent' event with commandName 'default'
gtag('consent', 'default', ...) appears after gtag('config', ...) in execution order
Tag Assistant shows 'Consent Mode not detected'

incorrect_consent_mode_configuration.description: Consent Mode implemented but with wrong default states or missing parameters
technical_indicators: ad_user_data or ad_personalization missing from consent default (v2 non-compliant)
Default state set to 'granted' instead of 'denied' for EU users
wait_for_update not set or set too low (CMP doesn't have time to initialize)
gcs parameter shows G111 before user interacts with banner

dark_pattern_implementations.description: CMP UI designed to steer users toward accepting
technical_indicators: Accept button has different CSS styling (larger, brighter color, higher contrast)
Reject option requires 2+ clicks while Accept requires 1 click
Pre-selected toggles in cookie preferences
Confusing button labels (e.g., 'Continue' meaning 'Accept All')
Banner positioned to obscure content, incentivizing quick acceptance

cookie_respawning.description: Cookies regenerated after user deletes them or withdraws consent
technical_indicators: Deleted cookies reappear after page navigation
localStorage or sessionStorage used to recreate cookies
Fingerprinting techniques used to recreate identifiers

cross_domain_consent_gaps.description: Consent collected on one domain but not enforced on subdomains or linked domains
technical_indicators: Different CMP configurations across subdomains
Consent cookie scope too narrow (not shared across subdomains)
Tracking active on subdomains without their own consent mechanism

legal_requirements: Supports GDPR, ePrivacy, CCPA/CPRA, LGPD and applicable regulations
IAB TCF v2.2 certified (for programmatic advertising compliance)
Google Certified CMP Partner (for Consent Mode integration)
Supports granular consent by purpose and vendor
Provides consent proof/audit trail (timestamp, version, choices)
Supports consent withdrawal as easily as granting
Configurable consent renewal periods by jurisdiction
banner_compliance: Accept and Reject buttons at same level with equal prominence
No dark patterns in UI design
Second layer with granular cookie-by-cookie controls
Accessible to screen readers (WCAG 2.2 Level AA)

must_support: Proper consent signal in dataLayer before any tags fire

tag_blocking_capability: Ability to block scripts BEFORE they execute (not just reactive signaling)
Support for blocking hardcoded scripts via script rewriting or placeholder patterns
Blocking of cookie-setting by non-managed scripts
cookie_scanning: Automatic periodic scanning of all cookies set by the website
Classification of cookies by category (technical, analytics, marketing, etc.)

loading_impact.good: CMP loads in <500ms, does not block page render, uses async loading
loading_impact.bad: CMP adds >1 second to page load, blocks rendering, large JS bundle (>200KB)
loading_impact.measurement: Measure with Lighthouse, WebPageTest. Check impact on LCP and CLS.

core_web_vitals_impact.cls: Banner should not cause layout shift. Use fixed positioning or reserved space. CLS impact should be <0.05.
core_web_vitals_impact.lcp: CMP script should not block LCP element rendering.

wcag_requirements: Screen reader compatible (proper ARIA labels, role attributes)
Sufficient color contrast (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text)
Focus management (focus trapped in modal if overlay, returned to trigger on close)
Buttons same size with comparable visual weight
Text readable without zoom up to 200%
common_accessibility_failures: Accept button visually larger/more prominent than Reject
Missing ARIA labels on consent toggles
Insufficient contrast on preference center UI

good_cmp_signs: Consent default fires BEFORE any tags
Network tab shows zero third-party tracking requests before consent
Rejecting all results in only essential cookies remaining
Consent state correctly reflected in gcs/gcd parameters
Consent audit trail stored and retrievable
bad_cmp_signs: Reject all still leaves marketing cookies
CMP adds >1 second to load time
Accept is visually dominant over Reject

10_consent_verification_testing_methodol.overview: Step-by-step technical methodology to verify consent implementation is working correctly. Use Chrome DevTools, Tag Assistant, and browser extensions.

browser_preparation: Use Chrome Incognito mode (or clear all site data)
Open DevTools (F12) BEFORE loading the page
Install extensions: Tag Assistant, DataLayer Checker, WASP (Web Analytics Solution Profiler)
Disable any ad blockers that might interfere with testing
tools_required: WASP or Omnibug (Chrome extension for tracking request inspection)

scenario_1_initial_page_load_no_interact.description: Load page without interacting with the consent banner
steps: 1. Open DevTools > Network tab, enable Preserve log
2. Load the page in incognito
3. DO NOT interact with the consent banner
4. Check Network tab: filter by domain (google-analytics, facebook, doubleclick, etc.)
5. Check Application > Cookies: look for _ga, _gid, _fbp, _gcl_au, _gcl_aw
6. Check Console: type 'dataLayer' and inspect for consent events

expected_results.network: No requests to analytics/advertising domains (Basic mode) OR only cookieless consent-state pings (Advanced mode)
expected_results.cookies: No _ga, _gid, _fbp, _gcl_au, _gcl_aw cookies present. Only CMP consent cookie and essential cookies.
expected_results.dataLayer: Should contain gtag consent default event with all parameters set to 'denied'. Example: {event: 'consent_default', ad_storage: 'denied', ...}

failure_indicators: ANY request to tracking domains before consent = FAIL
No consent default event in dataLayer = FAIL

scenario_2_reject_all.description: Click 'Reject All' on the consent banner
steps: 1. From Scenario 1 state, click 'Reject All'
2. Check Network tab for any NEW requests to tracking domains
3. Check Application > Cookies for any new non-essential cookies
4. Navigate to another page on the site
5. Repeat network and cookie checks
6. Check Console: dataLayer should show consent update with all 'denied'

expected_results.network: No new tracking requests after rejection. On page navigation, only consent-state pings (Advanced mode) or nothing (Basic mode).
expected_results.cookies: Only essential cookies + CMP preference cookie. No _ga, _fbp, etc.
expected_results.dataLayer: consent_update event with ad_storage:'denied', analytics_storage:'denied', ad_user_data:'denied', ad_personalization:'denied'
expected_results.gcs_parameter: Any Google requests should show gcs=G100

failure_indicators: New tracking cookies appear after reject = FAIL
Network requests to ad platforms after reject = FAIL
gcs shows G111 after reject = FAIL

scenario_3_accept_all.description: Click 'Accept All' on the consent banner
steps: 1. Clear all data, reload page in incognito
2. Click 'Accept All' on the consent banner
3. Check Network tab: tracking requests should now appear
4. Check Application > Cookies: analytics/advertising cookies should be set
5. Check Console: dataLayer should show consent update with 'granted'
6. Check gcs parameter in Google requests

expected_results.network: Requests to analytics and advertising domains now visible
expected_results.dataLayer: consent_update event with ad_storage:'granted', analytics_storage:'granted', ad_user_data:'granted', ad_personalization:'granted'

steps: 1. Clear all data, reload page
2. Open cookie preferences, enable only analytics, reject marketing
3. Check Network tab: only analytics requests should fire
4. Check cookies: only analytics cookies present, no advertising cookies
5. Check gcs parameter: should show G101 (analytics granted, ads denied)

scenario_5_consent_persistence.description: Verify consent choice persists across sessions
steps: 1. Accept all cookies
2. Close the browser tab
3. Reopen the site in a new tab (same browser, NOT incognito)
4. Verify: consent banner should NOT reappear
5. Verify: correct consent state is applied (check gcs, cookies, dataLayer)
1. Accept all cookies
2. Find and use the 'manage cookies' or 'privacy settings' link
3. Reject all / withdraw consent
4. Check that tracking cookies are removed
5. Check that tracking requests stop
6. Navigate to a new page and verify consent state is 'denied'
failure_indicators: No mechanism to withdraw consent = CRITICAL FAIL
Cookies not deleted after withdrawal = FAIL

network_tab.check_timing: Sort by 'Time' column. Any tracking request with timestamp before CMP interaction = non-compliant.
network_tab.check_gcs_in_url: Click on any Google request > check URL parameters for gcs= and gcd= values
network_tab.check_response_cookies: Click on request > Response Headers > look for Set-Cookie headers from tracking domains

application_tab.cookies_check: Application > Cookies > select domain > look for non-essential cookies
application_tab.local_storage_check: Application > Local Storage > check for tracking-related keys
application_tab.session_storage_check: Application > Session Storage > check for tracking-related keys

console_tab.check_consent_state: Type: google_tag_data?.ics?.entries — shows internal consent state if available

tools: Playwright/Puppeteer scripts for automated consent testing

playwright_test_concept.description: Automated test that loads page, checks for pre-consent tracking, clicks reject, and verifies no tracking persists
playwright_test_concept.pseudocode:
1. Launch browser with clean profile
2. Intercept network requests
3. Navigate to page
4. Assert: no tracking requests before CMP interaction
5. Click 'Reject All'
6. Navigate to second page
7. Assert: no tracking cookies present
8. Assert: no tracking network requests
9. Assert: gcs parameter = G100 in any Google requests
